1→# AGENTS.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Codex (Codex.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## What is Nurge
     6→
     7→Nurge is an outbound sales tool that learns from your sent email history. It runs as a Go CLI binary (`nurge`) that handles deterministic operations (sync, store, serve) while delegating intelligence (classify, converse, generate) to Codex via Codex skills.
     8→
     9→**Core principle:** Go binary = dumb (sync, store, serve). Codex = smart (classify, converse, generate).
    10→
    11→## Installation
    12→
    13→Users install via `curl` from the Cloudflare Worker at `install.nurge.sh`:
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash
    17→```
    18→
    19→The worker (`workers/install/`) proxies the install script from `usenurge/nurge` (public repo). After install, users run:
    20→
    21→```
    22→nurge my-outbound
    23→cd my-outbound
    24→```
    25→
    26→`nurge <directory>` creates the folder and initializes everything inside it. Running `nurge` with no argument initializes in the current directory (must be empty). Refuses to init in non-empty directories unless `--force` is passed. `nurge init` still works as a hidden alias.
    27→
    28→## Architecture: Two-Phase Setup
    29→
    30→### Phase A: `nurge` (Go binary, deterministic)
    31→Everything that doesn't need AI. One command, ~5 seconds, zero interaction:
    32→1. Create `.nurge/` directory and config files
    33→2. Create SQLite database
    34→3. Update `.gitignore`
    35→4. Install Codex hooks
    36→5. Install setup skill to `~/.Codex/skills/nurge-setup/`
    37→6. Git init + initial commit
    38→7. Print "run /nurge:setup" or launch Codex
    39→
    40→**Idempotent** — re-running skips completed steps.
    41→
    42→**Smart next-step detection:**
    43→- Inside Codex? → "Run /nurge:setup"
    44→- Codex not installed? → "Install Codex first"
    45→- Terminal with Codex available? → "Open Codex and run /nurge:setup"
    46→
    47→### Phase B: `/nurge:setup` (Codex skill, conversational)
    48→Everything that benefits from AI reasoning. Interactive conversation, ~5 minutes:
    49→1. Connect Gmail → Codex runs `nurge connect gmail`, guides through OAuth
    50→2. Connect CRM (optional) → asks if user has HubSpot, connects if yes
    51→3. Privacy consent → "Send email subjects to Codex? Continue?"
    52→4. Sync emails → `nurge sync` (all sent, last 90 days)
    53→5. Classify → Codex reads emails in batches, classifies, asks user about uncertain ones (~95% accuracy)
    54→6. Product discovery → "What's your website?" → WebFetch → confirms
    55→7. ICP conversation → Codex infers ICP from email patterns, confirms
    56→8. Analyze patterns → `nurge context generate` → presents insights
    57→9. DNS/health check → `dig TXT` + `nurge emails stats`
    58→10. Generate 13 skills → Codex writes project-local skills from data
    59→11. Summary + insights → "Try: write a cold email to the CTO at DataStack"
    60→
    61→## Conversation Learning System
    62→
    63→Nurge gets smarter with every Codex session. Users are GTM employees and founders — they don't use git, so Nurge must capture and version everything internally.
    64→
    65→### How it works
    66→1. **Capture** — after each Codex session, export the conversation into the DB (tone feedback, rejected drafts, ICP refinements, preferences)
    67→2. **Learn** — distill sessions into persistent learnings that skills reference
    68→3. **Improve** — skills get better with every conversation, not just every email sync
    69→
    70→### What gets captured
    71→- Tone preferences ("too salesy", "more casual", "shorter")
    72→- Rejected and accepted drafts
    73→- ICP refinements ("we don't sell to startups under 50 people")
    74→- Product positioning tweaks ("don't mention pricing in cold emails")
    75→- Competitor intel mentioned in conversation
    76→- Any explicit user corrections
    77→
    78→### Skill versioning
    79→Skills are stored as SKILL.md files on disk (for Codex to read), but **full versions are tracked in the DB**:
    80→- Content — the actual SKILL.md text
    81→- Version number — auto-incrementing per skill
    82→- Generated from — email count, classification state, learnings applied
    83→- Timestamp
    84→- Active flag — which version is currently on disk
    85→
    86→This lets Nurge roll back if a regeneration makes things worse, show what changed between versions, and know when skills are stale.
    87→
    88→### CLI commands
    89→```
    90→nurge learn save --stdin               # Skill self-reports session learnings
    91→nurge learn list --format json         # List all learnings
    92→nurge skills list --format json        # List skills with version info
    93→nurge skills history <name>            # Version history for a skill
    94→nurge skills rollback <name> <version> # Restore a previous skill version
    95→```
    96→
    97→## Skill ↔ CLI Interface
    98→
    99→All skill-to-binary communication is via CLI. No HTTP API, no direct DB access:
   100→
   101→```
   102→nurge account                          # Show auth status
   103→nurge account login                    # Force re-auth via browser
   104→nurge account logout                   # Revoke token + delete local auth
   105→nurge emails list --format json        # Skill reads data
   106→nurge emails classify --stdin          # Skill writes data
   107→nurge config show --format json        # Skill reads config
   108→nurge config set icp.titles "CTO,VP Eng"  # Skill writes config
   109→nurge context generate --format json   # Skill gets insights
   110→nurge send                             # Skill sends email
   111→nurge log --class outbound             # Classification filters
   112→nurge emails stats                     # Email statistics
   113→nurge emails check                     # Email health check
   114→nurge learn save --stdin               # Save session learnings
   115→nurge learn list --format json         # List learnings
   116→nurge skills list --format json        # List skills with versions
   117→nurge skills history <name>            # Skill version history
   118→nurge skills rollback <name> <version> # Restore previous version
   119→nurge connectors                       # List connected services
   120→nurge connectors available             # List all known connectors
   121→nurge connectors info <id> --format json  # Show connector details
   122→nurge connectors validate <file>       # Validate a connector YAML
   123→nurge connectors add --file <path>     # Add a custom connector YAML
   124→nurge connect <id>                     # Connect a service
   125→nurge disconnect <id>                  # Disconnect a service
   126→```
   127→
   128→## The 13 Generated Skills
   129→
   130→All written to `.Codex/skills/nurge-*/SKILL.md` (project-local, not global):
   131→
   132→| Category | Skills |
   133→|----------|--------|
   134→| Context  | `nurge-product`, `nurge-icp` |
   135→| Research | `nurge-research`, `nurge-qualify` |
   136→| Workflow | `nurge-pipeline` (orchestrates everything) |
   137→| Action   | `nurge-outbound`, `nurge-sequence`, `nurge-followup`, `nurge-replies`, `nurge-book` |
   138→| Health   | `nurge-hygiene`, `nurge-sending-health` |
   139→| Measure  | `nurge-analytics` |
   140→
   141→## Tech Stack
   142→
   143→### Go Binary
   144→- **CLI framework:** [cobra](https://github.com/spf13/cobra) with [viper](https://github.com/spf13/viper) for config
   145→- **Database:** SQLite via [mattn/go-sqlite3](https://github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3) (requires CGO_ENABLED=1 and gcc)
   146→  - Use WAL journal mode and `_busy_timeout` for concurrent access
   147→  - DSN format: `file:.nurge/nurge.db?_journal_mode=WAL&_busy_timeout=5000&_fk=true`
   148→- **Connectors** (YAML-defined, pluggable):
   149→  - Definitions in `internal/connector/definitions/*.yaml` (bundled) and `.nurge/connectors/definitions/*.yaml` (project-local)
   150→  - Auth methods: OAuth2, API key, basic auth, webhook — all handled by a generic engine
   151→  - Three tiers: Tier 1 (email, Go sync), Tier 2 (CRM, Codex-native), Tier 3 (enrichment, Codex-native)
   152→  - Adding a connector = dropping a YAML file, no Go code needed
   153→  - All tokens stored in `.nurge/connectors/<id>/token.json`
   154→- **Terminal UI (old wizard, superseded):** [charmbracelet/huh](https://github.com/charmbracelet/huh) — was used for the old setup wizard TUI forms. Keep huh only if needed for non-init interactive prompts.
   155→
   156→### Codex Integration
   157→- **Skills:** SKILL.md files with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `version`)
   158→  - Global skills: `~/.Codex/skills/nurge-setup/SKILL.md`
   159→  - Project skills: `.Codex/skills/nurge-*/SKILL.md`
   160→- **Hooks:** Configured in `.Codex/settings.json` under event keys (`PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `Stop`, `SessionStart`)
   161→  - Hook types: `command` (runs shell command) or `prompt` (injects prompt)
   162→  - Matcher patterns filter which tools trigger the hook
   163→- **Slash commands:** Markdown files with optional YAML frontmatter (`description`, `argument-hint`, `allowed-tools`)
   164→  - Support `$ARGUMENTS`, `$1`/`$2` positional args, `@path/to/file` references, `` !`command` `` bash execution
   165→
   166→## Build & Run
   167→
   168→```bash
   169→# Build
   170→go build -o nurge .
   171→
   172→# Run
   173→./nurge my-outbound
   174→./nurge sync
   175→./nurge emails list --format json
   176→
   177→# Test
   178→go test ./...
   179→go test ./internal/gmail/ -run TestSync -v    # single test
   180→
   181→# Lint
   182→golangci-lint run
   183→```
   184→
   185→## Project Layout
   186→
   187→```
   188→cmd/              # Cobra command definitions (root.go, init.go, sync.go, connect.go, emails.go, config.go, account.go, etc.)
   189→internal/         # Private packages
   190→  auth/           # Account auth — EnsureAuth, Login, Logout, Convex HTTP client, token file (~/.nurge/auth.json)
   191→  browser/        # Shared cross-platform browser.Open(url)
   192→  callbackserver/ # Shared local HTTP callback server for OAuth flows
   193→  connector/      # Connector interface, registry, shared OAuth flow, per-connector implementations
   194→  db/             # SQLite schema, migrations, queries
   195→  classifier/     # Email classification (outbound, internal, personal, etc.)
   196→  config/         # Config read/write (.nurge/config.json)
   197→  hooks/          # Codex hook installer
   198→  skills/         # Skill template generator
   199→  learning/       # Session learning capture, distillation, and retrieval
   200→.nurge/           # Project data directory (gitignored)
   201→  nurge.db        # SQLite database
   202→  config.json     # Project configuration
   203→  connectors/     # Per-connector auth state (gmail/, hubspot/)
   204→```
   205→
   206→## Key Design Decisions
   207→
   208→- **CLI-as-API:** Skills never touch the database directly. All data flows through `nurge` subcommands with `--format json`. This keeps the Go binary as the single source of truth.
   209→- **No HTTP server:** Everything is local CLI. No daemon, no ports, no API server.
   210→- **Idempotent init:** Every step in `nurge` checks if already done before executing. Safe to re-run.
   211→- **Old wizard is superseded:** The charmbracelet/huh-based setup wizard is replaced by the two-phase approach. Don't extend the old wizard.
   212→- **Learn from every session:** Users are GTM people, not developers. They won't commit or version anything. Nurge captures conversation learnings and skill versions in the DB automatically. Skills get smarter over time without user effort.
   213→- **DB owns versioning, not git:** Skill content lives on disk as SKILL.md (for Codex), but the DB tracks all versions, rollback history, and what data each version was generated from. Users never interact with git for this.
   214→

1→# Nurge Product Research
     2→
     3→This document summarizes Nurge based on the current repository implementation and supporting docs as of 2026-03-24.
     4→
     5→One important caveat: the repo currently has a few naming and scope inconsistencies across older docs and newer code. The biggest ones are:
     6→- Some files describe the AI layer as Claude Code, while higher-level repo guidance also talks about Codex. The implementation currently installs `.claude` hooks and skills.
     7→- Older docs describe 13 generated project-local skills, while the newer skill generation guide enumerates 16 project-local skills plus the global `nurge-setup` skill.
     8→
     9→## 1. Product Overview — what Nurge is
    10→
    11→Nurge is a local-first outbound sales workspace delivered as a Go CLI.
    12→
    13→Its core promise is simple: learn from a seller's sent email history and ongoing AI conversations, then turn that knowledge into repeatable outbound workflows. The product combines three layers:
    14→- A deterministic CLI for setup, storage, sync, sending, and sequencing
    15→- A local workspace that stores the user's outbound history and operating context
    16→- An AI layer that handles reasoning-heavy work such as classification, ICP discovery, research, drafting, and skill generation
    17→
    18→In plain English, Nurge is trying to become an outbound system that gets smarter every time the user emails, corrects a draft, refines their ICP, or runs a sales workflow.
    19→
    20→## 2. User Journey — step-by-step from install to daily use
    21→
    22→### Install and bootstrap
    23→
    24→1. The user installs Nurge from the hosted install endpoint:
    25→   `curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash`
    26→2. The installer fetches `install.sh` through a Cloudflare Worker that proxies the script from GitHub.
    27→3. The user creates a workspace with either:
    28→   `nurge my-outbound`
    29→   or `nurge` inside an empty directory.
    30→
    31→### Phase A: deterministic project setup
    32→
    33→4. The CLI initializes the local project by:
    34→   - creating `.nurge/`
    35→   - creating `config.json`
    36→   - creating the SQLite database
    37→   - updating `.gitignore`
    38→   - installing `.claude/settings.json` hooks
    39→   - installing the global `nurge-setup` skill
    40→   - initializing git and making the first commit
    41→5. If Claude Code is installed, Nurge launches it automatically. If not, it prints the next step.
    42→
    43→### Connect tools and bring in data
    44→
    45→6. On the first authenticated command, the user logs into their Nurge account in the browser.
    46→7. The user connects Gmail with `nurge connect gmail`.
    47→8. The user can optionally connect HubSpot with `nurge connect hubspot`.
    48→9. The user syncs sent-mail history with `nurge sync`, which pulls sent Gmail messages, stores them locally, and builds a contact mirror.
    49→
    50→### Phase B: AI-guided setup
    51→
    52→10. The user runs `/nurge:setup` inside the AI environment.
    53→11. The setup skill:
    54→    - confirms connector state
    55→    - gathers company and website context
    56→    - learns role, team size, and maturity
    57→    - sets config values
    58→    - classifies and interprets outbound history
    59→    - infers or confirms product positioning, ICP, personas, and KPIs
    60→    - generates project-local skills tailored to that workspace
    61→12. From that point on, the workspace is personalized to how the user actually sells.
    62→
    63→### Daily use
    64→
    65→13. The user researches targets, imports leads, or inspects the lead mirror.
    66→14. The user drafts and sends one-off outbound emails or starts sequence enrollments.
    67→15. Nurge tracks follow-up tasks, reply events, send attempts, suppressions, and mailbox policy.
    68→16. The user checks health and analytics, adjusts campaigns/templates/mailbox policy, and keeps going.
    69→17. Session hooks and learning commands capture feedback so the next AI-assisted workflow is more accurate.
    70→
    71→## 3. Core Capabilities — what the product can do
    72→
    73→### Workspace and setup
    74→
    75→- Create a fully initialized outbound workspace from a single CLI command
    76→- Install AI hooks and setup skills automatically
    77→- Re-run setup idempotently
    78→
    79→### Data capture and CRM-like memory
    80→
    81→- Sync sent Gmail history into a local SQLite database
    82→- Mirror contacts from outbound activity
    83→- Store optional CRM data such as deals
    84→- Persist conversation learnings, drafts, and skill history
    85→
    86→### Configuration and personalization
    87→
    88→- Store project configuration in `.nurge/config.json`
    89→- Learn product positioning, ICP, personas, and KPI settings
    90→- Generate personalized project-local AI skills
    91→
    92→### Outbound execution
    93→
    94→- Send manual emails through Gmail
    95→- Start and manage sequence enrollments
    96→- Define reusable sequence templates
    97→- Create campaigns that bind sequences to default mailboxes
    98→- Dispatch due follow-ups according to mailbox policy
    99→
   100→### Reply and task management
   101→
   102→- Infer or persist follow-up tasks
   103→- Sync inbound replies on active threads
   104→- Classify replies like interested, bounced, unsubscribe, not interested, or referral
   105→- Pause, stop, reopen, draft, and resolve reply-needed work
   106→
   107→### Safety, governance, and deliverability
   108→
   109→- Maintain suppression lists for emails and domains
   110→- Track mailbox caps, warmup state, rotation groups, send windows, and reputation
   111→- Run deliverability-style health views, including DNS-based checks
   112→
   113→### Learning and iteration
   114→
   115→- Save learnings from AI sessions
   116→- Track versions of generated skills
   117→- Roll skills back if a new generation is worse
   118→- Sync selected local and execution state to Convex for account-level visibility
   119→
   120→## 4. Technical Architecture — how it's built (plain English)
   121→
   122→Nurge is built as a hybrid local app plus synced backend.
   123→
   124→### Local CLI layer
   125→
   126→The main product is a Go binary built with Cobra. That binary owns the command surface and handles predictable tasks:
   127→- project initialization
   128→- auth bootstrapping
   129→- connector flows
   130→- sync
   131→- local persistence
   132→- send execution
   133→- sequence state management
   134→- hook handling
   135→
   136→### Local data layer
   137→
   138→Each workspace gets a `.nurge/` directory. That folder holds the local config, connector state, and SQLite database. SQLite runs in WAL mode with a busy timeout, which is a good fit for a single-user local tool that still needs durable state and occasional concurrent access.
   139→
   140→### Connector layer
   141→
   142→Connectors are defined through a generic registry and YAML-backed definitions. Today the first-class product integrations are Gmail and HubSpot, but the registry is designed so additional connectors can be added without rewriting the whole CLI.
   143→
   144→### AI integration layer
   145→
   146→The current implementation installs hooks into `.claude/settings.json` and writes skills as `SKILL.md` files. Those skills do not talk to the database directly. Instead, they call `nurge` subcommands as their interface to the system.
   147→
   148→This is the product's most important architectural boundary:
   149→- the CLI is the source of truth for state changes
   150→- the AI layer is the source of truth for reasoning-heavy decisions
   151→
   152→### Cloud and account layer
   153→
   154→Nurge also includes a Convex backend. It is used for account auth, token exchange, and synced execution/dashboard state. The repo also contains a Cloudflare Worker that serves the install script.
   155→
   156→## 5. Data & Storage — how data flows and persists
   157→
   158→### Main data flow
   159→
   160→1. External systems provide source data.
   161→   Gmail provides sent mail and reply threads.
   162→   HubSpot provides CRM context when connected.
   163→2. The CLI stores and updates that data locally.
   164→3. AI reads the local state through CLI commands, not direct SQL.
   165→4. The user sends emails and advances sequences through the CLI.
   166→5. Session hooks capture conversation context and checkpoints.
   167→6. Convex sync pulls shared execution state down and pushes local learnings back up.
   168→
   169→### Files and local storage
   170→
   171→- `.nurge/config.json`
   172→  Workspace configuration, connector metadata, setup state, and user-defined settings.
   173→- `.nurge/nurge.db`
   174→  Main local SQLite database.
   175→- `.nurge/connectors/<id>/token.json`
   176→  Per-connector auth state.
   177→- `.nurge/sessions/tmp`
   178→  Session-related local scratch space.
   179→- `~/.nurge/auth.json`
   180→  Nurge account auth token for the local machine.
   181→- `.claude/settings.json`
   182→  Installed hook configuration.
   183→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/`
   184→  Global setup skill.
   185→- `.claude/skills/nurge-*/`
   186→  Project-local generated skills.
   187→
   188→### Major database domains
   189→
   190→- Connector and sync state:
   191→  `connectors`
   192→- Email and contact memory:
   193→  `emails`, `contacts`, `deals`
   194→- Learning and skill memory:
   195→  `learnings`, `skill_versions`
   196→- Session capture:
   197→  `sessions`, `session_events`, `session_checkpoints`, `content_drafts`
   198→- Sequencing runtime:
   199→  `mailboxes`, `sequence_enrollments`, `follow_up_tasks`, `sequence_templates`, `campaigns`, `mailbox_send_events`, `send_attempts`, `sequence_drafts`
   200→- Safety and reply operations:
   201→  `suppressions`, `reply_events`
   202→
   203→### Local-first with selective cloud sync
   204→
   205→The current sync engine is hybrid rather than purely local or purely cloud.
   206→
   207→Cloud-synced execution tables currently include:
   208→- emails
   209→- mailboxes
   210→- mailbox send events
   211→- sequence enrollments
   212→- follow-up tasks
   213→- suppressions
   214→- reply events
   215→- sequence drafts
   216→
   217→Local-authored state pushed upward includes:
   218→- contacts
   219→- learnings
   220→- skill versions
   221→- deals
   222→- email classification changes
   223→
   224→So the product is best described as local-first with a synced execution backend, not as an offline-only tool.
   225→
   226→## 6. Integrations — what external services it connects to
   227→
   228→### Current first-class integrations
   229→
   230→- Gmail
   231→  Required for the main product loop. Used for OAuth, sent-mail sync, direct send, and reply tracking.
   232→- HubSpot
   233→  Optional CRM enrichment. Used to add company, contact, and deal context.
   234→- Convex
   235→  Account auth, token exchange, and state synchronization.
   236→- Cloudflare Workers
   237→  Hosts the install endpoint that proxies the install script.
   238→
   239→### Supporting system integrations
   240→
   241→- Local browser + callback server
   242→  Used to complete login and OAuth flows.
   243→- DNS tooling (`dig`)
   244→  Used by health-oriented flows for DNS/auth checks.
   245→
   246→### Extensibility path
   247→
   248→The connector registry already supports bundled and project-local YAML definitions, so the architecture is prepared for more connector types such as enrichment, sequencing, or webhook integrations. That said, the honest current product surface is still Gmail first and HubSpot second.
   249→
   250→## 7. Intelligence Layer — how Claude/AI fits in
   251→
   252→The AI layer is where Nurge becomes more than a mail sync tool.
   253→
   254→### What AI is responsible for
   255→
   256→AI handles the parts of outbound work that are ambiguous, contextual, or judgment-heavy:
   257→- discovering product positioning from website and conversation context
   258→- inferring or refining ICP and personas
   259→- helping classify email history
   260→- researching prospects and companies
   261→- qualifying leads
   262→- drafting outbound emails, follow-ups, and replies
   263→- generating project-local skills that encode the user's process
   264→- capturing and reusing session learnings
   265→
   266→### How AI is wired into the product
   267→
   268→- A global setup skill routes onboarding and maturity-aware setup.
   269→- Project-local skills cover product knowledge, ICP, research, qualifying, outbound, sequencing, replies, analytics, and related workflows.
   270→- Session hooks capture starts, prompts, stops, and ends so the system can remember what happened in each AI session.
   271→- Skills use CLI commands as their API boundary instead of hitting the database directly.
   272→
   273→### Why this matters
   274→
   275→This separation lets Nurge keep deterministic systems stable while still improving the most important sales logic over time. The same seller can gradually teach the system:
   276→- tone preferences
   277→- what counts as a good lead
   278→- which segments to avoid
   279→- how to handle objections
   280→- which drafts feel on-brand
   281→
   282→### Important repo note
   283→
   284→The implementation currently expresses this through Claude Code hooks and `.claude` skill paths, even though some repo guidance also refers to Codex. Architecturally, the meaning is the same: the agent layer is the reasoning engine, and the CLI is the operational engine.
   285→
   286→## 8. CLI Command Reference — every command and what it does
   287→
   288→### Project, bootstrap, and built-ins
   289→
   290→| Command | What it does |
   291→|---|---|
   292→| `nurge [directory]` | Main entrypoint. Initializes a new Nurge workspace in a new folder, or initializes the current empty directory. |
   293→| `nurge init [directory]` | Hidden compatibility alias for the same initialization flow. |
   294→| `nurge version` | Prints the installed Nurge version/build information. |
   295→| `nurge help [command]` | Shows command help. |
   296→| `nurge completion <shell>` | Generates shell completion for supported shells. |
   297→
   298→Common init flags:
   299→- `--force` reruns init steps
   300→- `--skip-hooks` skips hook installation
   301→- `--verbose` increases init output
   302→
   303→### Account and cloud sync
   304→
   305→| Command | What it does |
   306→|---|---|
   307→| `nurge account` | Shows whether the user is logged into their Nurge account. |
   308→| `nurge account login` | Starts browser-based Nurge account login. |
   309→| `nurge account logout` | Revokes the local account session and removes the saved token. |
   310→| `nurge pull` | Pulls latest synced execution state from Convex into local SQLite. |
   311→| `nurge push` | Pushes local state such as learnings, contacts, skill versions, deals, and classification updates to Convex. |
   312→
   313→### Connectors and configuration
   314→
   315→| Command | What it does |
   316→|---|---|
   317→| `nurge connect <connector>` | Connects a service such as Gmail or HubSpot. |
   318→| `nurge disconnect <connector>` | Removes a connected service and deletes its local token state. |
   319→| `nurge connectors` | Lists currently connected services. |
   320→| `nurge connectors available` | Lists all bundled and project-local connector definitions known to the registry. |
   321→| `nurge connectors validate <file>` | Validates a connector YAML definition file. |
   322→| `nurge connectors info <id>` | Shows structured details for a connector definition. |
   323→| `nurge connectors add --file <path>` | Installs a project-local connector YAML definition into the workspace. |
   324→| `nurge config` | Parent command for configuration operations. |
   325→| `nurge config show` | Prints the current workspace config. |
   326→| `nurge config set <key> <value>` | Sets a config value, including setup markers and user settings. |
   327→
   328→### Email history and context
   329→
   330→| Command | What it does |
   331→|---|---|
   332→| `nurge sync` | Syncs sent Gmail history into the local database. |
   333→| `nurge emails` | Parent command for synced-email operations. |
   334→| `nurge emails list` | Lists synced emails, optionally filtered by class. |
   335→| `nurge emails stats` | Summarizes volume, date range, and classification counts. |
   336→| `nurge emails classify` | Accepts JSON on stdin and writes email classifications back into the database. |
   337→| `nurge emails check` | Shows simple sending-health metrics from email history. |
   338→| `nurge log` | Shortcut view over email history, mainly as a filtered email log. |
   339→| `nurge context` | Parent command for outbound context generation. |
   340→| `nurge context generate` | Analyzes email patterns and produces outbound insights. |
   341→
   342→### Sending, leads, and suppression
   343→
   344→| Command | What it does |
   345→|---|---|
   346→| `nurge send` | Sends an email via Gmail. It can also create a first-touch sequence enrollment or advance a queued task, depending on flags. |
   347→| `nurge leads` | Parent command for local lead-mirror operations. |
   348→| `nurge leads list` | Lists contacts and their current outbound/sequence state. |
   349→| `nurge leads import` | Imports leads from a CSV file into the local contact mirror. |
   350→| `nurge suppress` | Parent command for suppression-list management. |
   351→| `nurge suppress list` | Lists suppressed emails and domains. |
   352→| `nurge suppress add <email-or-domain>` | Adds an email or domain to the suppression list. |
   353→| `nurge suppress remove <email-or-domain>` | Removes a suppression entry. |
   354→| `nurge sends` | Parent command for send-ledger inspection. |
   355→| `nurge sends list` | Lists recorded send attempts and their outcomes. |
   356→
   357→### Sequences, campaigns, tasks, replies, and mailbox policy
   358→
   359→| Command | What it does |
   360→|---|---|
   361→| `nurge sequence` | Parent command for sequence enrollment and template operations. |
   362→| `nurge sequence list` | Lists current sequence enrollments. |
   363→| `nurge sequence templates` | Lists available sequence templates. |
   364→| `nurge sequence templates add` | Adds or updates a custom sequence template from JSON. |
   365→| `nurge sequence templates delete <name>` | Deletes a custom sequence template. |
   366→| `nurge sequence pause <enrollment-id>` | Pauses an active sequence enrollment. |
   367→| `nurge sequence resume <enrollment-id>` | Resumes a paused sequence enrollment. |
   368→| `nurge campaigns` | Parent command for campaign defaults attached to outbound sequences. |
   369→| `nurge campaigns create <name>` | Creates or updates a campaign and binds it to a sequence, optionally with a default mailbox. |
   370→| `nurge campaigns list` | Lists campaigns and their status. |
   371→| `nurge campaigns pause <name>` | Pauses a campaign. |
   372→| `nurge campaigns resume <name>` | Resumes a paused campaign. |
   373→| `nurge tasks` | Parent command for follow-up task operations. |
   374→| `nurge tasks list` | Lists persisted or inferred follow-up tasks. |
   375→| `nurge tasks snooze <task-id>` | Pushes a task into the future. |
   376→| `nurge tasks cancel <task-id>` | Cancels a task. |
   377→| `nurge dispatcher` | Parent command for due-task dispatch evaluation. |
   378→| `nurge dispatcher run` | Evaluates due tasks against mailbox policy and can optionally execute ready sends. |
   379→| `nurge replies` | Parent command for reply tracking and reply queue management. |
   380→| `nurge replies sync` | Scans active Gmail threads for inbound replies and records them. |
   381→| `nurge replies list` | Lists recorded reply events. |
   382→| `nurge replies queue` | Lists reply events that still need human or AI action. |
   383→| `nurge replies done <reply-id>` | Marks a reply-needed item as handled. |
   384→| `nurge replies reopen <reply-id>` | Reopens a previously handled reply item. |
   385→| `nurge replies draft <reply-id>` | Stores a draft response on a reply item. |
   386→| `nurge mailboxes` | Parent command for mailbox policy and health views. |
   387→| `nurge mailboxes status` | Shows mailbox usage, caps, and remaining capacity. |
   388→| `nurge mailboxes health` | Shows mailbox deliverability-style health, including optional DNS checks. |
   389→| `nurge mailboxes set-policy <mailbox-email>` | Updates mailbox send policy, warmup state, windows, caps, or rotation settings. |
   390→
   391→### Analytics, learning, and skill versioning
   392→
   393→| Command | What it does |
   394→|---|---|
   395→| `nurge analytics` | Parent command for outbound analytics. |
   396→| `nurge analytics summary` | Shows a high-level sequencer dashboard by sequence and mailbox. |
   397→| `nurge learn` | Parent command for conversation-learning operations. |
   398→| `nurge learn save` | Saves a learning from stdin into the database. |
   399→| `nurge learn list` | Lists stored learnings, optionally filtered by type. |
   400→| `nurge skills` | Parent command for generated skill tracking. |
   401→| `nurge skills list` | Lists skills with active versions and update info. |
   402→| `nurge skills history <skill-name>` | Shows historical versions of a specific skill. |
   403→| `nurge skills rollback <skill-name> <version>` | Restores a previous skill version. |
   404→
   405→### Hidden/internal commands
   406→
   407→| Command | What it does |
   408→|---|---|
   409→| `nurge hook` | Hidden parent command for AI hook handlers. |
   410→| `nurge hook post-tool-use` | Internal hook entrypoint after tool use. |
   411→| `nurge hook session-start` | Builds session banner/context and records session start data. |
   412→| `nurge hook user-prompt` | Records a submitted user prompt. |
   413→| `nurge hook stop` | Records a stop/checkpoint event. |
   414→| `nurge hook session-end` | Marks a session as ended. |
   415→
   416→

1→# Skill Catalog
     2→
     3→All 17 Nurge skills organized by category. Each entry covers what the skill does, what data it reads, what commands it runs, when it's used, and how it behaves at different maturity levels.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Setup Skill (Global)
     8→
     9→### nurge-setup
    10→
    11→**Location:** `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/SKILL.md` (global, installed to user's home directory)
    12→
    13→**Purpose:** The onboarding orchestrator. Runs the full two-phase setup process that connects services, gathers user data, and generates all other skills.
    14→
    15→**What it does:**
    16→- Checks prerequisites (project initialized, services connected)
    17→- Handles resume logic (can pick up where it left off if interrupted)
    18→- Runs Phase 1: Foundation (7 steps collecting name, company, role, tools, email connection, sync, maturity detection)
    19→- Detects maturity level and delegates to the appropriate Phase 2 flow
    20→- Phase 2 collects ICP, personas, positioning, KPIs, and generates all 16 project skills
    21→- Ends with a live demo showing the product in action
    22→
    23→**When it's used:** Once, during initial setup. Invoked by the user running `/nurge:setup` in Claude Code.
    24→
    25→**Not regenerated:** This is the only skill that stays fixed. It's installed by the Go binary during `nurge init` and updated when Nurge is updated.
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## Context Skills (3)
    30→
    31→These are the knowledge base. They hold static information about the user's company, target market, and buyer personas that all other skills reference.
    32→
    33→### nurge-product
    34→
    35→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-product/SKILL.md`
    36→
    37→**Purpose:** Everything Claude needs to know about the user's product to write accurate outbound emails.
    38→
    39→**What it contains:**
    40→- Company name, website, and industry
    41→- One-line product description for cold outreach
    42→- Value propositions (what problems it solves)
    43→- Key features and differentiators
    44→- Competitive positioning
    45→- Pricing model (if known)
    46→
    47→**Config it reads:** `company.website`, `company.description`, `company.industry`, `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`
    48→
    49→**How it varies by maturity:**
    50→- Zero/Experimenting: Built from website scrape and setup conversation. May be thin initially.
    51→- Operating/Scaling: Built from website scrape plus how the user actually describes the product in their sent emails. Often more grounded than the website copy — reveals what resonates with prospects.
    52→
    53→**When it's used:** Every time Claude drafts any outbound communication. It's the foundational product knowledge that all action skills reference.
    54→
    55→---
    56→
    57→### nurge-icp
    58→
    59→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-icp/SKILL.md`
    60→
    61→**Purpose:** Defines the ideal customer profile — which companies to target.
    62→
    63→**What it contains:**
    64→- Company size ranges (employees, revenue)
    65→- Industries and verticals
    66→- Company stage (startup, growth, enterprise)
    67→- Geography
    68→- Technographics (tech stack signals, if known)
    69→- Negative filters (who NOT to target)
    70→
    71→**Config it reads:** `icp.*`, `prospects.geography`
    72→
    73→**How it varies by maturity:**
    74→- Zero/Experimenting: Built from user assumptions and AI suggestions. Starts narrow (one segment) and expands as data comes in. Acknowledges uncertainty.
    75→- Operating/Scaling: Inferred from real email patterns — who they actually email vs. who responds vs. who converts. Often reveals misalignment between the user's stated targets and their actual best-performing segments.
    76→
    77→**When it's used:** By nurge-research for targeting, nurge-prospects for list building, nurge-qualify for fit scoring, and nurge-analytics for segment analysis.
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→### nurge-personas
    82→
    83→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-personas/SKILL.md`
    84→
    85→**Purpose:** Defines buyer personas — which people to message within target companies, and how to message each one differently.
    86→
    87→**What it contains (for each of 2-3 personas):**
    88→- Title and role patterns
    89→- What they care about (pain points, priorities)
    90→- Messaging angle (what resonates with this persona)
    91→- Tone preferences (technical vs. business, formal vs. casual)
    92→- Common objections
    93→- Whether they're a champion, decision-maker, or blocker
    94→
    95→**Config it reads:** `personas` (JSON array)
    96→
    97→**How it varies by maturity:**
    98→- Zero/Experimenting: Built from ICP and product type with AI suggestions. User confirms or edits.
    99→- Operating/Scaling: Extracted from email patterns — which titles they email most, which respond best, what messaging they use per role. Includes real reply rate data per persona.
   100→
   101→**When it's used:** By nurge-outbound to tailor email voice, nurge-sequence to vary cadence messaging, nurge-research to know who to look for at target companies, and nurge-analytics for per-persona performance breakdowns.
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→## Research Skills (2)
   106→
   107→These handle the intelligence-gathering phase before any outreach happens.
   108→
   109→### nurge-research
   110→
   111→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-research/SKILL.md`
   112→
   113→**Purpose:** Deep-dive research on a target company or prospect before writing any outreach.
   114→
   115→**What it does:**
   116→- Builds a complete prospect dossier using a multi-layered lookup strategy
   117→- Finds the right person at the target company (matching buyer personas)
   118→- Gets their contact info (email, phone)
   119→- Surfaces what they care about, recent activity, and trigger events
   120→- Detects competitive intel (are they using a competitor?)
   121→- Presents the dossier for user review before handing off to drafting
   122→
   123→**Research Strategy (5 parallel layers):**
   124→
   125→1. **Web Search (context layer)** — Person name + company for role and background. Company name + news for funding, hiring, product launches, trigger events. Cross-references results to avoid wrong-person errors.
   126→
   127→2. **Company Website (contact layer)** — Fetches the company site for positioning and team info. Geography-aware legal pages are goldmines for contact info:
   128→   - France: `/mentionslegales` or `/mentions-legales` (legally required — founder email, phone, registration)
   129→   - Germany/Austria: `/impressum` (legally required — managing director, email, phone)
   130→   - US/UK: `/about`, `/contact`, `/team` pages
   131→
   132→3. **Enrichment APIs (structured data layer)** — Optional integrations with ContactOut, Apollo, Clay for verified email, phone, and work history. The skill works without them but they add verified data.
   133→
   134→4. **Public Profiles (personality layer)** — LinkedIn posts (via web search), Twitter/X, GitHub, personal blogs, podcast appearances, conference talks. Reveals how the person thinks and communicates.
   135→
   136→5. **Prior Interactions (history layer)** — Checks whether the user has emailed this person or company before, and what happened.
   137→
   138→All 5 layers run as parallel agents simultaneously, then combine into a structured dossier.
   139→
   140→**The dossier includes:**
   141→- Person: name, role, background, what they care about
   142→- Contact: email (verified or best guess), phone, LinkedIn URL
   143→- Company: what they do, size, stage, recent news, trigger events
   144→- Messaging angle: based on persona match and what this specific person cares about
   145→- Confidence level: flags low-confidence findings
   146→
   147→**Config it reads:** `icp.*`, `personas`, `product.positioning`, `prospects.geography`
   148→
   149→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json` (check prior interactions)
   150→
   151→**Critical rule:** Always present research to the user for review before drafting. Wrong info in a personalized email is worse than a generic email.
   152→
   153→---
   154→
   155→### nurge-qualify
   156→
   157→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-qualify/SKILL.md`
   158→
   159→**Purpose:** Evaluate whether a lead is worth pursuing and classify their relationship type.
   160→
   161→**What it does (two-step process):**
   162→
   163→**Step 1 — Classify the lead:**
   164→
   165→| Classification | Meaning | Next Action |
   166→|---|---|---|
   167→| Prospect | Fits ICP, could be a customer | Continue to qualification scoring |
   168→| Competitor | Sells the same or similar thing | Flag and disqualify. Never email. Log for competitive intel. |
   169→| Partnership | Complementary product or service | Flag as potential partner. Route to partnership messaging, not sales. |
   170→
   171→Classification compares what the prospect's company does vs. what the user's company does: same space + same buyer = competitor; same space + different buyer = partnership; different space + matches ICP = prospect.
   172→
   173→**Step 2 — For prospects, run the qualification framework:**
   174→- Apply BANT (or custom framework) from config
   175→- Score fit against ICP criteria
   176→- Check persona match
   177→- Assess buying and timing signals
   178→- Check for existing relationship (prior emails)
   179→- Recommend: pursue (strong fit + timing), nurture (good fit, bad timing), or pass (poor fit)
   180→
   181→**Config it reads:** `qualification.framework`, `icp.*`, `personas`, `company.description`, `company.industry`
   182→
   183→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json` (prior interaction history)
   184→
   185→**How it varies by maturity:**
   186→- Zero/Experimenting: Uses BANT or the framework set during setup. Conservative — defaults to "nurture" when unsure.
   187→- Operating/Scaling: Framework informed by pipeline data (deal velocity, win rates) if CRM is connected.
   188→
   189→**When it's used:** After research, before committing to a sequence. Prevents wasting sends on bad-fit prospects, competitors, and misrouted partnerships.
   190→
   191→---
   192→
   193→## Workflow Skill (1)
   194→
   195→### nurge-pipeline
   196→
   197→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-pipeline/SKILL.md`
   198→
   199→**Purpose:** The orchestrator. Manages the full outbound workflow and coordinates all other skills.
   200→
   201→**What it does:**
   202→- Manages prospect pipeline stages: new, researched, qualified, sequencing, replied, meeting, deal
   203→- Decides which skill to invoke at each stage
   204→- Tracks where every prospect is in the funnel
   205→- Suggests next actions ("You have 5 prospects waiting for follow-up, 3 new companies to research")
   206→- Enforces daily sending limits from KPI targets
   207→
   208→**Config it reads:** `kpi.*`, `sending.*`
   209→
   210→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list`, `nurge emails stats`, `nurge config show`
   211→
   212→**When it's used:** When the user asks "what should I do today?" or "show me my pipeline." The starting point for a typical outbound session.
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## Action Skills (5)
   217→
   218→These do the actual work — writing emails, managing sequences, handling replies, and booking meetings.
   219→
   220→### nurge-outbound
   221→
   222→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-outbound/SKILL.md`
   223→
   224→**Purpose:** Write cold outbound emails. This is the highest-priority action skill.
   225→
   226→**What it does:**
   227→- Drafts personalized cold emails using product knowledge, ICP, persona-specific messaging, and research findings
   228→- Enforces a strict 50-90 word limit. Words are counted before presenting. If over 90, cut ruthlessly. No exceptions.
   229→- Follows a rigid structure: 1 personalized hook (1 sentence) then 1 value prop (1-2 sentences) then 1 CTA (1 sentence). Nothing else.
   230→- Eliminates all filler: no "I hope this finds you well", no company backstory, no feature lists, no "I'd love to", no "I was wondering if"
   231→- Matches tone to persona (technical for CTOs, business for VPs)
   232→- If social proof exists in config: weaves ONE relevant proof point naturally. Never fabricates proof.
   233→- If no social proof exists: skips entirely — never invents it.
   234→- Includes compliance requirements based on prospect geography (unsubscribe for CAN-SPAM, physical address if required)
   235→- Presents draft with word count for user review before sending
   236→
   237→**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `product.social_proof`, `personas`, `prospects.geography`, `sending.*`
   238→
   239→**CLI commands:** `nurge send --to <email> --subject <s> --body <b>`
   240→
   241→**Key principle:** Never sends without user approval. Always presents the draft first.
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→### nurge-sequence
   246→
   247→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-sequence/SKILL.md`
   248→
   249→**Purpose:** Design and manage multi-step outreach cadences.
   250→
   251→**What it does:**
   252→- Creates sequences: first touch (Day 1), follow-up (Day 3), final nudge (Day 7)
   253→- Varies messaging per step — each touch uses a new angle, not "just following up"
   254→- Adapts sequence length to persona and deal complexity
   255→- Tracks sequence progress per prospect
   256→- Suggests templates based on performance data
   257→
   258→**Config it reads:** `personas`, `kpi.*`, `sending.*`
   259→
   260→**How it varies by maturity:**
   261→- Zero/Experimenting: Generates simple 3-touch sequences based on best-practice frameworks.
   262→- Operating/Scaling: Sequences informed by real performance data — what cadence patterns actually get replies.
   263→
   264→---
   265→
   266→### nurge-followup
   267→
   268→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-followup/SKILL.md`
   269→
   270→**Purpose:** Write follow-up emails for prospects who haven't replied.
   271→
   272→**What it does:**
   273→- Drafts follow-ups that add new value (never "just checking in")
   274→- References the original email naturally
   275→- Escalates the angle: different pain point, new social proof, or a more direct ask
   276→- Respects sequence timing
   277→- Knows when to stop (after final touch, marks as "no response")
   278→
   279→**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `personas`
   280→
   281→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json` (pull original thread), `nurge send`
   282→
   283→---
   284→
   285→### nurge-replies
   286→
   287→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-replies/SKILL.md`
   288→
   289→**Purpose:** Handle incoming replies — positive, negative, objections, or referrals.
   290→
   291→**What it does:**
   292→- Classifies reply intent: interested, objection, not now, not interested, referral, out-of-office
   293→- Drafts the appropriate response for each type:
   294→  - Interested: move to booking
   295→  - Objection: address with relevant value prop
   296→  - Not now: schedule nurture follow-up
   297→  - Not interested: graceful exit, respect the no
   298→  - Referral: thank and follow up with the referral
   299→- Flags tone issues (if reply is angry, approaches carefully)
   300→
   301→**Config it reads:** `product.positioning`, `personas`, `qualification.framework`
   302→
   303→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json`, `nurge send`
   304→
   305→---
   306→
   307→### nurge-book
   308→
   309→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-book/SKILL.md`
   310→
   311→**Purpose:** Convert positive replies into booked meetings.
   312→
   313→**What it does:**
   314→- Proposes specific times (not "when works for you?")
   315→- Handles timezone differences
   316→- Drafts a booking confirmation email
   317→- Prepares a meeting brief: company research, persona context, talking points, potential objections
   318→- Sends reminders before the meeting
   319→- Handles reschedules and no-shows gracefully
   320→
   321→**Config it reads:** `personas`, `product.positioning`
   322→
   323→**CLI commands:** `nurge send`
   324→
   325→---
   326→
   327→## Prospect Management Skill (1)
   328→
   329→### nurge-prospects
   330→
   331→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-prospects/SKILL.md`
   332→
   333→**Purpose:** Build and manage prospect lists. For users without a CRM, this IS their CRM.
   334→
   335→**What it does:**
   336→- Builds targeted prospect lists from ICP and persona criteria
   337→- Segments lists by persona, industry, company size, geography
   338→- Tracks prospect status: new, researched, qualified, contacted, replied, meeting, deal, passed
   339→- Manages suppression: who to stop emailing (opted out, bounced, bad fit, already a customer)
   340→- Deduplicates contacts
   341→- Imports from CSV if user has existing lists
   342→- Surfaces prospects that need attention
   343→
   344→**Config it reads:** `icp.*`, `personas`, `prospects.geography`
   345→
   346→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list --format json`, `nurge emails stats`
   347→
   348→**How it varies by maturity:**
   349→- Zero/Experimenting: This is critical — Nurge is their only system for managing prospects. Must handle list building, segmentation, and status tracking end to end.
   350→- Operating/Scaling: Less critical if they have a CRM, but still useful for Nurge-specific tracking and for users whose CRM isn't yet supported.
   351→
   352→**When it's used:** "Build me a list of 50 CTOs at Series B SaaS companies" or "who haven't I followed up with?" or "show me my prospect pipeline."
   353→
   354→---
   355→
   356→## Health Skills (2)
   357→
   358→These protect the user's sending reputation and data quality.
   359→
   360→### nurge-hygiene
   361→
   362→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-hygiene/SKILL.md`
   363→
   364→**Purpose:** Keep data clean and outbound compliant.
   365→
   366→**What it does:**
   367→- Flags stale prospects (no activity in X weeks)
   368→- Identifies bounced and invalid emails
   369→- Checks for duplicate contacts
   370→- Enforces suppression lists (unsubscribes, bounces)
   371→- Verifies email formats before sending
   372→- Flags compliance issues based on prospect geography
   373→- Suggests list cleanup actions
   374→
   375→**Config it reads:** `prospects.geography`
   376→
   377→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails list`, `nurge emails stats`
   378→
   379→**When it's used:** Periodically — triggered by nurge-pipeline or nurge-review. Also runs before any bulk send.
   380→
   381→---
   382→
   383→### nurge-sending-health
   384→
   385→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-sending-health/SKILL.md`
   386→
   387→**Purpose:** Monitor and manage email sending infrastructure, including warmup.
   388→
   389→**What it does:**
   390→- Checks DNS records (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) and flags issues with fix instructions
   391→- Monitors sending volume against daily limits
   392→- Tracks bounce rates and spam complaint signals
   393→- Manages warmup schedule for fresh domains:
   394→  - Week 1-2: 5 emails/day
   395→  - Week 3-4: 10-15 emails/day
   396→  - Week 5+: 20-30 emails/day (if health stats are clean)
   397→  - Auto-reduces if bounce rate spikes
   398→- Recommends dedicated outbound domain if user sends from their primary domain
   399→- Alerts when something looks wrong
   400→
   401→**Config it reads:** `sending.domain_type`, `sending.mailbox_count`, `sending.warmup_status`, `kpi.daily_emails`
   402→
   403→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails stats`, `dig TXT` (DNS checks)
   404→
   405→**Future enhancement:** When warmup service connectors are added (Instantly, MailReach), this skill will pull real reputation data and auto-adjust limits based on actual reputation scores instead of time-based schedules.
   406→
   407→---
   408→
   409→## Measure Skills (2)
   410→
   411→These track performance and help the user improve over time.
   412→
   413→### nurge-analytics
   414→
   415→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-analytics/SKILL.md`
   416→
   417→**Purpose:** Track outbound performance against KPI targets.
   418→
   419→**What it does:**
   420→- Dashboard: emails sent, reply rate, positive replies, meetings booked vs. KPI targets
   421→- Breakdown by persona, segment, and sequence
   422→- Trend analysis: is performance improving or declining?
   423→- Surfaces best and worst performing: subject lines, personas, send times, sequences
   424→- Compares against benchmarks from the setup KPI conversation
   425→- Funnel analysis: sent to replied to meeting to deal conversion rates
   426→
   427→**Config it reads:** `kpi.*`
   428→
   429→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails stats`, `nurge emails list --format json`, `nurge context generate --format json`
   430→
   431→**How it varies by maturity:**
   432→- Zero/Experimenting: Limited to basic volume metrics and reply tracking.
   433→- Operating/Scaling: Full breakdown by persona, segment, sequence, send time, and subject line patterns.
   434→
   435→**When it's used:** "How am I doing?" or "show me my analytics" or triggered by nurge-review during periodic check-ins.
   436→
   437→---
   438→
   439→### nurge-review
   440→
   441→**Location:** `.claude/skills/nurge-review/SKILL.md`
   442→
   443→**Purpose:** Periodic coaching — weekly or monthly review of outbound performance with actionable advice.
   444→
   445→**What it does:**
   446→- Pulls KPI progress: "This week you sent X emails, got Y replies, booked Z meetings (target was W)"
   447→- Highlights wins: "Your CTO emails are getting 18% reply rate — above target"
   448→- Flags problems: "VP Eng reply rate dropped to 3% — let's adjust the messaging"
   449→- Compares current period to previous period
   450→- Suggests specific actions: increase CTO volume, rewrite VP Eng sequences, try a new subject line angle
   451→- Updates KPIs if current targets are too easy or too hard
   452→- Feeds insights back into other skills (adjust persona messaging, ICP focus)
   453→
   454→**Config it reads:** `kpi.*`, `user.name`, `personas`
   455→
   456→**CLI commands:** `nurge emails stats`, `nurge context generate --format json`, `nurge learn list --format json`
   457→
   458→**How it varies by maturity:**
   459→- Zero/Experimenting: Lighter reviews focused on getting started and building habits.
   460→- Operating: Full KPI review with data-driven recommendations.
   461→- Scaling: Includes per-rep breakdowns showing who's hitting targets and who needs help, with best-practice sharing from top performers.
   462→
   463→**When it's used:** User asks "give me a weekly review" or nurge-pipeline suggests it's time for a check-in.
   464→

   482→
   483→## Skill Interaction Map
   484→
   485→Skills don't operate in isolation. Here's how they reference each other:
   486→
   487→- **nurge-pipeline** orchestrates all other skills and decides which to invoke
   488→- **nurge-research** feeds into **nurge-qualify** which feeds into **nurge-outbound**
   489→- **nurge-outbound** produces emails that **nurge-sequence** manages as multi-step cadences
   490→- **nurge-followup** handles non-responses within sequences
   491→- **nurge-replies** classifies responses and routes interested replies to **nurge-book**
   492→- **nurge-prospects** provides the lead lists that **nurge-research** investigates
   493→- **nurge-hygiene** and **nurge-sending-health** protect deliverability for all sending skills
   494→- **nurge-analytics** and **nurge-review** measure results and suggest improvements to all other skills
   495→- **nurge-product**, **nurge-icp**, and **nurge-personas** are referenced by nearly every other skill as the knowledge foundation
   496→

1→---
     2→name: nurge-setup
     3→description: Interactive setup for nurge — understands your company, sets outbound KPIs, connects tools, and generates personalized skills. Run after `nurge`.
     4→version: 4.0.0
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Nurge Setup (Router)
     8→
     9→You are helping the user set up nurge for outbound sales. This is a conversational onboarding — ask one question at a time, be warm and direct, use the user's name throughout.
    10→
    11→**This file is the router.** It runs prerequisites, checks resume state, runs Phase 1, detects maturity, and delegates to the right Phase 2 file.
    12→
    13→## Prerequisites
    14→
    15→1. Confirm `nurge` has been run (check for `.nurge/` directory). If missing, tell the user to run `nurge` first.
    16→2. Run `nurge connectors` to see what's already connected.
    17→
    18→## Resume Logic
    19→
    20→Setup can be interrupted and resumed. Before starting, check what's already configured:
    21→
    22→Run: `nurge config show --format json`
    23→
    24→### If `setup_completed` is true:
    25→Say: "[Name], setup is already complete. What would you like to do?"
    26→Do NOT re-run setup.
    27→
    28→### If `maturity.level` is set:
    29→Phase 1 is done. Read the right phase-2 file and continue from there.
    30→Say: "[Name], welcome back! We figured out where you are — let me pick up from there."
    31→Then: Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-{maturity.level}.md` and follow it.
    32→
    33→### If `maturity.level` is NOT set:
    34→Phase 1 is incomplete. Check Phase 1 config keys and skip completed steps.
    35→Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-1-foundation.md` and follow it.
    36→
    37→### Phase 1 Resume Keys
    38→
    39→| Config key exists | Skip step |
    40→|-------------------|-----------|
    41→| `user.name` | F1 |
    42→| `company.website` | F2 |
    43→| `user.role` AND `team.size` | F3 |
    44→| `tools.crm` | F4 |
    45→| `email.connected` or Gmail in `nurge connectors` | F5 |
    46→| `detection.outbound_count` | F6 |
    47→| `maturity.level` | F7 (entire detection) |
    48→
    49→If resuming mid-Phase 1, greet with: "[Name], welcome back! Let me pick up where we left off."
    50→
    51→## Flow
    52→
    53→1. Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-1-foundation.md` and follow it (steps F1–F7)
    54→2. Phase 1 ends with maturity detection (F7) — the level is saved to `maturity.level`
    55→3. Based on the detected level, read the corresponding Phase 2 file:
    56→   - `zero` → Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-zero.md`
    57→   - `experimenting` → Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-experimenting.md`
    58→   - `operating` → Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-operating.md`
    59→   - `scaling` → Read `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-scaling.md`
    60→4. Follow the Phase 2 file exactly.
    61→
    62→## Important Notes
    63→
    64→- **One question at a time.** Never dump multiple questions. This is a conversation, not a form.
    65→- **Use their name.** Make it personal throughout.
    66→- **Be honest about limitations.** Only email channel. Only Gmail + HubSpot connectors. Don't oversell.
    67→- **Save everything to config.** Every decision → `nurge config set`. Skills read config, not session logs.
    68→- **Session hooks capture the conversation automatically.** No extra work needed for learning.
    69→- **Show only summaries during classification.** Don't show raw classification tables — just the summary count.
    70→
    71→## Current Capabilities (Be Honest)
    72→
    73→- **Channels:** Email only. LinkedIn and phone on roadmap.
    74→- **Connectors:** Gmail (Tier 1) and HubSpot (Tier 2) only.
    75→- Be upfront about these limits. Don't ask about what we can't deliver.
    76→

1→# Outbound Signals Reference
     2→
     3→Signals are data points that tell you whether a prospect is worth reaching out to, when, and how. Nurge's research and qualification skills use these signals to build prospect dossiers and score leads.
     4→
     5→**Key principle:** Most signals are findable via web search and native Claude tools (WebSearch, WebFetch). No paid APIs required for the core signal set. Enrichment connectors (ContactOut, Apollo, Clay) add structured data on top.
     6→
     7→**Parallel execution:** When researching a prospect, Claude should dispatch multiple agents in parallel — one per signal category. Each agent runs its own web searches independently and reports back. This turns a 5-minute serial research task into a 30-second parallel one.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Signal Categories
    12→
    13→### 1. Buying Signals (They Might Need What You Sell)
    14→
    15→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    16→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    17→| **Recently raised funding** | New budget, growth mandate, likely hiring and buying tools | WebSearch: "[company] funding round 2025 2026", Crunchbase, TechCrunch, company blog |
    18→| **Hiring for relevant roles** | Building a team = buying tools for that team | WebSearch: "[company] hiring [role]", company careers page (WebFetch /careers), LinkedIn jobs, Greenhouse/Lever pages |
    19→| **Job posts mention your problem space** | Explicit signal they have the pain you solve | WebSearch: "[company] jobs [keyword from your product]", Indeed, LinkedIn |
    20→| **Tech stack changes** | Migrating or adopting new tech = buying window | WebSearch: "[company] migrates to [tech]", BuiltWith, StackShare, GitHub repos |
    21→| **Headcount growth** | Growing company = growing needs | WebSearch: "[company] headcount growth", LinkedIn company page (via search) |
    22→| **New leadership** | New CTO/VP often brings new tools, new strategy | WebSearch: "[company] new CTO/VP/Head of", LinkedIn (via search) |
    23→| **Expanding to new markets** | New geo/vertical = new infrastructure needs | WebSearch: "[company] expansion", press releases, company blog |
    24→| **Product launch** | Shipping something new = needs around that launch | WebSearch: "[company] launches", Product Hunt, company blog |
    25→| **Partnerships announced** | Signals growth and strategic direction | WebSearch: "[company] partnership", press releases |
    26→| **Pain signals in reviews** | Users complaining about problems you solve | WebSearch: "site:g2.com [company]", "site:glassdoor.com [company]", Capterra reviews |
    27→| **Competitor churn** | They're leaving a competitor = active buyer | WebSearch: "[company] switches from [competitor]", G2 reviews mentioning competitors |
    28→| **RFP/procurement signals** | Actively buying | WebSearch: "[company] RFP [category]", government procurement sites |
    29→
    30→### 2. Timing Signals (Right Moment to Reach Out)
    31→
    32→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    33→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    34→| **Fiscal year start** | New budget cycle = spending authority | WebSearch: "[company] fiscal year", SEC filings for public companies |
    35→| **End of quarter** | Budget pressure to spend remaining allocation | Calendar-based (Q1: Jan-Mar, Q2: Apr-Jun, etc.) |
    36→| **Contract renewal period** | Evaluating alternatives | Hard to find externally — infer from when they adopted current tool |
    37→| **Competitor incident** | Their current vendor failed them | WebSearch: "[competitor] outage/breach/issue", Twitter/X, Hacker News |
    38→| **Industry regulation change** | Compliance deadline = forced buying | WebSearch: "[industry] regulation 2026", industry publications |
    39→| **Conference/event season** | Decision-makers are thinking about the space | WebSearch: "[industry] conference 2026", event websites |
    40→| **Recent leadership change** | New leader = 90-day window to make changes | WebSearch: "[company] appoints [role]", LinkedIn |
    41→| **Recent layoffs** | May be looking to do more with less (efficiency tools) | WebSearch: "[company] layoffs", LinkedIn |
    42→
    43→### 3. Fit Signals (They Match Your ICP)
    44→
    45→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    46→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    47→| **Company size** | Within your ICP range | WebSearch: "[company] employees", LinkedIn company page, Crunchbase |
    48→| **Industry/vertical** | Matches your target market | Company website (WebFetch), LinkedIn, Crunchbase |
    49→| **Revenue range** | Can afford your product | WebSearch: "[company] revenue", Crunchbase, SEC filings, Inc 5000, Deloitte Fast 500 |
    50→| **Growth stage** | Startup/growth/enterprise matches your sweet spot | Crunchbase (funding rounds), LinkedIn headcount, press coverage volume |
    51→| **Geography** | Within your target regions | Company website, LinkedIn, WebSearch |
    52→| **Technology used** | Uses tech your product integrates with or replaces | BuiltWith (WebFetch), StackShare, GitHub, job postings (tech requirements) |
    53→| **Business model** | B2B/B2C/SaaS/marketplace — matches your target | Company website (WebFetch) |
    54→| **Customer profile** | Who THEY sell to tells you about their sophistication | Company website, case studies, G2 profile |
    55→
    56→### 4. Persona Signals (About the Individual)
    57→
    58→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    59→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    60→| **Recent job change** | New role = looking to make impact, open to new tools | WebSearch: "[person] joins [company]", LinkedIn (via search) |
    61→| **Published content** | Know what they think about, how they communicate | WebSearch: "[person] blog/article/podcast", LinkedIn posts (via search), Medium, Substack |
    62→| **Conference speaker** | Thought leader, influences decisions | WebSearch: "[person] speaker [conference]", YouTube, event sites |
    63→| **Open source contributor** | Technical, values transparency | GitHub profile, WebSearch: "[person] github" |
    64→| **Social media activity** | What they share = what they care about | WebSearch: "[person] twitter/linkedin", LinkedIn posts (via search) |
    65→| **Book/course author** | Deep expertise in specific area, known authority | WebSearch: "[person] book/course", Amazon |
    66→| **Board/advisory roles** | Influential, connected | WebSearch: "[person] advisor/board", LinkedIn (via search) |
    67→| **Education background** | Shared connections, communication style | LinkedIn (via search), university alumni pages |
    68→| **Mutual connections** | Warm intro potential | Hard via web search — better with LinkedIn Sales Nav (future connector) |
    69→
    70→### 5. Risk Signals (Don't Reach Out / Proceed with Caution)
    71→
    72→| Signal | What It Means | Where to Find It |
    73→|--------|--------------|------------------|
    74→| **Competitor** | They sell the same thing you do | WebSearch: "[company] vs [your product]", company website (WebFetch), G2 category |
    75→| **Already a customer** | Don't cold email your own customers | `nurge emails list --format json`, CRM data if connected |
    76→| **Previously rejected** | Respect the no — or wait significant time | `nurge emails list --format json` (check for negative replies) |
    77→| **Unsubscribed/bounced** | Legal obligation to not contact | Suppression list in nurge-hygiene |
    78→| **Company in trouble** | Layoffs, bankruptcy, acquisition = not buying | WebSearch: "[company] layoffs/bankruptcy/acquired", news |
    79→| **Bad domain reputation** | Their emails might not reach you either | DNS check, but usually not relevant for outbound |
    80→| **Recently contacted by team** | Don't double-tap from multiple people | `nurge emails list --format json`, CRM data |
    81→| **Litigation/controversy** | Risky to associate with | WebSearch: "[company] lawsuit/controversy" |
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→## Parallel Research Architecture
    86→
    87→When researching a prospect, Claude should dispatch **parallel agents** — one per signal category. This is the key to fast, thorough research.
    88→
    89→### Agent Dispatch Pattern
    90→
    91→```
    92→User: "Research Acme Corp / Jane Smith"
    93→         │
    94→         ├─→ Agent 1: COMPANY SIGNALS
    95→         │     WebSearch: "[company] funding"
    96→         │     WebSearch: "[company] hiring"
    97→         │     WebSearch: "[company] news 2026"
    98→         │     WebFetch: company website
    99→         │     WebFetch: company careers page
   100→         │
   101→         ├─→ Agent 2: PERSON SIGNALS
   102→         │     WebSearch: "[person] [company]"
   103→         │     WebSearch: "[person] speaker conference"
   104→         │     WebSearch: "[person] blog article"
   105→         │     WebSearch: "site:linkedin.com [person] posts"
   106→         │
   107→         ├─→ Agent 3: FIT + RISK CHECK
   108→         │     Compare company against ICP (from config)
   109→         │     Check competitor match (from config)
   110→         │     Check prior emails (nurge emails list)
   111→         │     WebSearch: "[company] vs [our product]"
   112→         │
   113→         ├─→ Agent 4: CONTACT INFO
   114→         │     WebSearch: "[person] email [company]"
   115→         │     WebFetch: company /contact or /mentions-legales or /impressum
   116→         │     WebSearch: "site:contactout.com [person]"
   117→         │     WebSearch: "site:rocketreach.co [person]"
   118→         │
   119→         └─→ Agent 5: TIMING SIGNALS
   120→               WebSearch: "[company] Q1 2026 plans"
   121→               WebSearch: "[competitor of their current tool] issues"
   122→               Check fiscal year / industry events
   123→```
   124→
   125→### All agents return → Combine into Dossier
   126→
   127→```
   128→Prospect Dossier: Jane Smith, CTO @ Acme Corp
   129→──────────────────────────────────────────────
   130→FIT:      Strong match (Series B, 120 employees, SaaS, US)
   131→RISK:     None detected (not a competitor, no prior contact)
   132→BUYING:   Just raised $15M Series B (2 months ago), hiring 3 engineers
   133→TIMING:   New CTO (started 4 months ago), fiscal year starts Jan
   134→PERSONA:  Spoke at DevConf 2025 on "scaling engineering teams",
   135→          posts about developer productivity, values transparency
   136→CONTACT:  jane@acme.com (from careers page), LinkedIn: /in/janesmith
   137→ANGLE:    Reference her DevConf talk on scaling → your product helps
   138→          engineering teams ship faster → relevant to her $15M growth mandate
   139→
   140→CONFIDENCE: High (multiple corroborating sources)
   141→```
   142→
   143→### Present to user for review before drafting.
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Signal Sources Summary
   148→
   149→| Source | Free? | What It Gets You | How to Access |
   150→|--------|-------|-----------------|---------------|
   151→| **Google (WebSearch)** | Yes | Everything — the starting point for all signals | WebSearch tool |
   152→| **Company website** | Yes | Product info, team, careers, legal pages (contact info) | WebFetch tool |
   153→| **LinkedIn posts** | Yes | What prospects think about, recent activity | WebSearch: "site:linkedin.com [person]" |
   154→| **LinkedIn profile** | Blocked | Direct scrape fails (403/999) | Use web search to get profile snippets |
   155→| **Crunchbase** | Partial | Funding, investors, company info | WebSearch (basic), WebFetch (some pages) |
   156→| **G2** | Yes | Reviews, competitor comparisons, pain signals | WebSearch: "site:g2.com [company]" |
   157→| **Glassdoor** | Yes | Company culture, leadership sentiment | WebSearch: "site:glassdoor.com [company]" |
   158→| **GitHub** | Yes | Tech stack, open source activity, contributor profiles | WebSearch/WebFetch |
   159→| **Product Hunt** | Yes | Product launches, tech adoption | WebSearch: "site:producthunt.com [company]" |
   160→| **Twitter/X** | Partial | Real-time signals, opinions, announcements | WebSearch: "site:twitter.com [person/company]" |
   161→| **News (TechCrunch, etc.)** | Yes | Funding, launches, leadership changes | WebSearch |
   162→| **Job boards** | Yes | Hiring signals, tech stack from requirements | WebSearch, WebFetch careers pages |
   163→| **SEC/EDGAR** | Yes | Revenue, financials (public companies only) | WebSearch, WebFetch |
   164→| **Mentions Légales** | Yes | Contact info (French companies, legally required) | WebFetch: [company].fr/mentionslegales |
   165→| **Impressum** | Yes | Contact info (German companies, legally required) | WebFetch: [company].de/impressum |
   166→| **ContactOut** | Paid | Verified email + phone from LinkedIn URL | Future connector |
   167→| **Apollo** | Freemium | Email, company data, technographics | Future connector |
   168→| **Clay** | Paid | Waterfall enrichment from multiple sources | Future connector |
   169→| **BuiltWith** | Freemium | Technology stack detection | WebFetch (basic), future connector |
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→## How Skills Use Signals
   174→
   175→| Skill | Which Signals | How |
   176→|-------|--------------|-----|
   177→| **nurge-research** | All of them — builds the full dossier | Dispatches parallel agents per category |
   178→| **nurge-qualify** | Fit + Risk + Buying | Scores against ICP, checks competitor, checks BANT |
   179→| **nurge-outbound** | Persona + Timing + Buying | Uses signals to personalize the email angle |
   180→| **nurge-sequence** | Timing | Determines sequence cadence and urgency |
   181→| **nurge-followup** | Buying + Timing | References trigger events in follow-ups |
   182→| **nurge-review** | All (aggregated) | Surfaces which signal types correlate with replies |
   183→
